title: Sea Stories
original_prose:
I’d do it again, if I had the chance.

—Sturgill Simpson, “Sea Stories”

####

Obedience is the first proof of worth. All else is noise.

—SynRgy Directive 440.7: Guardian Youth Doctrine

####

The dust didn’t rise when they marched us in. It hung. Suspended. Like the air itself had stopped moving out of respect or fear. Rows of boys, maybe fifty. Maybe more. None younger than ten. None older than sixteen. All of us stripped of our names, but not yet our reasons.

I had one. Her name was Mae.

I hadn't seen her since they pulled her away. Didn't know if she was alive, dead, or—like the older boys liked to joke—sent to a farm. I didn’t fully understand what that meant, only that it had something to do with babies, and it was bad enough no one ever laughed when they said it.

We were lined up in the yard of a transit facility. Fenced, sun-bleached, broken speakers wired to dead light poles. The kind of place that used to be a middle school. You could still see the mural. A cartoon eagle clutching books and a flag. It had fangs now.

That’s when I saw it. The trucks. Four of them. Big, silent, armored transports crawling out of what used to be the gymnasium. The roof had collapsed on one end. The scoreboard still hung above it, flickering 0–0. Canvas-backed. No insignia. Just black. Moving slow, like they had time. Like they owned it.

The one at the end had a flap open. Just for a second. Just long enough.

Her face. Pale. Watching. Mae.

She didn’t cry. Didn’t scream. She just stared through the slit in the tarp like she’d already said goodbye a thousand times. And then—she dropped something.

The damn rabbit.

Small. Dirty. Stuffed. One ear mended with red thread. I remembered that stitch. I’d done it with a shoelace. Years ago.

The rabbit hit the dirt just inside the fence line.

I didn’t move. Couldn’t. Not until the trucks were gone, swallowed by the haze.

Then I stepped forward. One pace. Two.

A guard shifted. Raised his weapon.

I didn’t look at him. Just stopped short. Let the rabbit sit there in the dust.

Somewhere nearby, the guards were murmuring to each other. I couldn’t hear what they said, but I wondered—what were they betting on? Which one of us would snap first? How many would die before the gate opened?

Someone laughed behind me. A boy with cracked lips and hollow eyes. He said something I didn’t catch.

Didn’t matter.

A voice crackled over the speakers. This one worked.

The screen mounted above the fence buzzed to life—old, sun-burnt, color faded to piss and dust. A man in a SynRgy uniform stared blankly into the camera. Not a commander. Not a priest. Just some middle-tier functionary with too many medals and not enough soul. His eyes were dead. His tone was worse.

"Attention, candidates. Welcome to your sacred trial. Today marks the beginning of your refinement. Of your devotion. The strong will be shaped. The obedient will be lifted. Those who endure will serve Lord Voss directly, in the ranks of the Guardian Core.

This is not merely a path of selection. This is a sacrament of purpose. A gift of potential. A fire in which your impurities will be burned away—if you are worthy.

You will walk to SynRgy One."

We all groaned. SynRgy One—that was Las Vegas, right? As far as I knew, it was on another planet. Walk there?

"The path is sacred. The journey is your first obedience. Your first proof.

Only one per route will be chosen. The others will serve in ways appropriate to their worth—biological, structural, spiritual.

Prove yourselves faithful.

Begin."

That was the moment it started. Not the walk. The killing.

A silence fell—thick and wet and wrong. One boy whimpered. Another started to pray, whispering into cracked hands. Someone else threw up and didn’t even wipe their mouth. A smaller kid clutched the back of an older one’s shirt and begged him not to move.

None of it mattered.

Three older boys turned immediately—like they’d just been waiting for the excuse. One drove his elbow into the throat of the kid next to him. Another pulled a shiv from his waistband and gutted someone before they could scream.

No one told them to. They already knew. A camera drone lifted off the roof. Wings humming. One of the guards saluted it, grinning. The others laughed and started calling out numbers like they were watching a pit fight.

I looked down the line and saw Eli.

He saw me too. Eyes wide. He tried to run to me—but didn’t make it far. He tripped, hard, face-first into the dirt. His shoelaces were tied together. On purpose, maybe. Or maybe someone just thought it’d be funny.

I didn’t think. I ran to him, dropped to my knees, jerked the laces apart like they’d been stitched to bone, and yanked him upright.

"Thanks," he mumbled, dazed.

That’s when one of the bigger boys swung. A blade flashed low. I twisted, caught it along the ribs. Felt the burn. Didn’t stop.

We bolted.

The gate wasn’t locked. But there was a guard posted there now. Smirking. Baton raised. Like he was hoping someone would try.

I hit him in the groin with everything I had. He folded, gagging. I didn’t wait. Grabbed the sidearm off his belt—didn’t even know if it was loaded—just felt the weight and kept going.

We were almost through when I stopped.

The rabbit.

Still lying in the dust. Still watching.

I turned back. Scooped it up. Pressed it to my chest.

That’s when Benny came at me.

He wasn’t running. Just walking fast. Blade still bloody. Grinning like the whole thing was sport.

I raised the pistol. My hands were trembling.

Eli fumbled at the gate controls. "Almost—almost got it—"

Benny stopped just short. Eyes locked on mine.

"This isn’t over," he said.

Then he turned and strolled back into the yard. Toward the blood. Toward the game.

I didn’t look back.

Eli and I left.



####

We tried to go home first.

Galveston was south. That’s where we ran. No plan. Just instinct. We stuck to the brush and the shallow water, figuring we could double back, hide in the ruins. Maybe find some old crawlspace to die in that didn’t belong to SynRgy.

Didn’t make it half a day.

The drone came out of nowhere. Big. Loud. Black glass and sharp angles. We heard the whine first—then the air split. Gunfire tore through the sand ten meters ahead. A warning. The next wouldn’t be.

We hit the dirt. Waited. Watched the thing circle once and then vanish west.

Eli looked at me. “They’re not gonna let us go, are they?”

I didn’t answer.

I pulled the pistol. Checked it. Still had weight. Still felt real.

“I can make it quick,” I said. “If you want.”

He blinked. Thought about it. Then shook his head.

“It’s nice today,” he said. “Let’s enjoy the walk.”

And that was that.



####

We stayed just off I-10. Close enough to follow the waypoints burned into our implants, but never on the road. Too exposed. The others were out there. Behind us. Hunting, maybe. Or just hungry.

The days were blistered. The sun never blinked. Shade was a myth. Skin peeled whether you moved or not. Wind carried ash like it was pollen.

But Eli laughed. He talked. He pointed out clouds and lizards and the shape of burnt trees that looked like dogs.

He made me laugh, too. Not much. But enough to remember I still could.

One night we found a pond. Half-dry, stinking, but wet enough. We stripped and swam under the stars—real stars. The kind that hadn’t yet shut down. I left the rabbit on a rock. Almost forgot it. Panicked. Found it again.

We stole food from a boarded-up farmhouse. Cans. Jerky. A jar of pickled eggs that tasted like rubber and god.

We made a fire once. Just once. Just to watch it. Not for warmth. Not for cooking. Just to see something burn that wasn’t us.

Eli asked questions.

“What was your favorite color?”

I didn’t have one.

“Do you think the stars are still up there?”

I didn’t know.

“Do you think there’s another way to Vegas?”

“No,” I said. “There’s only forward.”

And we kept going.

Every mile was a little more gone. The land. The sky. Us.

But somehow—it was the freest I’d ever felt.

And I hated that.



####

Time stopped keeping track.

The sun didn’t rise anymore. It just returned, like a threat. Heat didn’t build—it landed. Full force. First thing in the morning. Like it had been waiting all night to strike.

We stopped counting days somewhere in New Mexico. The ground had stopped pretending to be soil by then. It was glass in places. Melted. Smeared across old medians like fingerprints on a body that didn’t want to be found.

Eli got quieter.

Still smiled. Still hummed. But the questions stopped. And he didn’t say why.

We passed a boy strung up against a billboard once—arms out, chest peeled. There was a sign nailed above him: “Left the path.”His eyes were still open.

That night we didn’t speak at all.

We followed the shape of I-10, always nearby. A ghost highway. You could feel it more than see it—heat radiating off fractured lanes, glass from old windshields ground into the shoulder like sand. We saw smoke behind us once. Big. Distant. But moving. The other boys, maybe. Or what was left of them.

I started getting nosebleeds. Eli’s cough got wetter. The water we drank probably wasn't good for us. We hit the edge of El Paso, or what used to be El Paso. The city was empty. Not quiet—empty. Buildings stripped for wire. Vehicles fused into slag from solar flares. Street signs half-melted, still bearing SynRgy emblems. No people. No sound. Just the wind dragging plastic bags like ghosts.

They weren’t evacuated. They were harvested.

“Do you think it was all for the grid?” Eli asked once. “The Spark?”

I didn’t answer. But we passed a small chapel on the way out, door kicked in, altar scorched. Something had bled there—maybe a person. Maybe not. The ground was still black.

Later, we saw what was left of a SynRgy relay station. Burned out. Tagged with the letters FSA in blood or paint. A makeshift noose hung from a gutted drone rig.

Someone had taken it back. Or tried.

We found a gas station half-sunken into the asphalt. Eli scavenged for water. I found a freezer full of rot. There was something curled up on the floor, burned beyond shape. Meat, maybe. Human. I didn’t tell him.

Our bodies had changed. My beard itched. Eli’s hair had curled over his ears. I’d lost more weight than I could afford. Our clothes no longer fit right. My boots bled my feet. His toenails blackened and peeled.

In the distance—finally—we saw something.

A signal tower. SynRgy’s shape, glowing red even through the heat. And beyond that: the mirage of lights. Vegas.

We were close. Not safe. Not clean. But close.

That night, we camped low in a dry culvert beneath a shattered overpass. No fire. Just canned beans and silence. The stars were hiding, and the air felt electric—like something was wrong before it happened.

Then we heard it.

Voices. Laughter. Cracking branches maybe thirty meters off.

Eli tensed. I snuffed the lamp.

We waited.

Two shapes moved into the treeline. One tall. One heavier. Familiar. The big one barked something low. The tall one laughed. We couldn’t hear the words—but we knew the cadence. The weight.

Benny.

And the other one—the boy who’d beat on Eli in the yard, back at the start.

They dropped packs. Built a fire. One of them opened a bottle—god knows where he got it. The other leaned in too close. Said something Benny didn’t like.

There was a struggle. Quick. Brutal.

Then Benny stood alone. Blade wet in his hand.

We didn’t move.

He looked around once. Then grabbed his gear and jogged off—west, toward Vegas.

He was ahead of us now.

And we knew—we weren’t just running to something anymore.

We were being hunted.



####

Vegas looked nothing like I imagined.

It shimmered on the horizon like it was drowning. Towers rising from red dust, spires wrapped in chrome and scripture. The walls were high. Clean. Wrong. They didn’t reflect the light—they absorbed it.

A checkpoint stood in front. Concrete slabs, sensor rigs, half a dozen SynRgy guards in bone-white armor, heads uncovered so we could see they were still human.

One of them stepped forward, checking a pad. “Two entries. One clearance.”

Eli turned to me before I could speak.

“I know,” he said. Calm. Too calm. “It was always going to be you.”

“No,” I said. “Shut the fuck up. Don’t—”

“It’s okay,” he whispered. “You’re the one who remembers.”

I shook my head. “We’re not doing this.”

“We are. We have to.”

The guards didn’t rush. They’d seen this before. Maybe they liked it. Maybe it was part of the show.

Eli stepped forward, placed the rabbit in my hands. “Keep this safe. For Mae.”

“Eli—”

He reached for the pistol still tucked in my belt. I didn’t stop him. He pulled it, flipped it, and handed it to me grip-first.

“You’ll make it quick,” he said.

I hated him for that.

“I don’t want to,” I said. My voice cracked.

“I know. That’s why it has to be you.”

He turned. Stepped forward. Knelt.

My hand trembled.

“Do it,” he said. “It’s a good day to die.”

The guards watched.

I pulled the trigger.

The shot echoed like a memory that never stops screaming.

I was still kneeling when I heard footsteps behind me.

“Touching,” Benny said.

I turned. Too slow.

He hit me hard. I hit the ground. The pistol skidded away.

“You think you’re better than me?” he spat.

I didn’t answer. Just swung.

He dodged. Kicked my ribs. I heard something crack.

“You stole my kill,” he said, pulling his knife.

I grabbed a rock. Didn’t think. Just swung upward.

It caught him across the temple. He stumbled. I tackled him.

We rolled. Bit. Clawed. He stabbed me in the shoulder. I screamed. He laughed.

I grabbed the knife with both hands. Pushed.

Slowly.

Into him.

He didn’t scream. Just looked surprised.

Then he stopped moving.

For a moment, I didn’t either. I looked down—at him, at myself. Blood everywhere. Face slack. My own reflection shimmered in the visor of a nearby drone. I looked like one of them. Worse.

The guards didn’t help. Didn’t stop it. One of them wrote something on a clipboard. Another murmured, almost bored, “Efficient.”

I stood up. Blood-soaked. Barely breathing.

“Clearance confirmed,” the guard said.

The gate opened.

I didn’t look back.

I stepped inside.

Twelve hundred miles. We made it. And for what?

That was the last time I ever felt anything real.
